title: To Seek the Good of the Church
speaker: Pastor Renan de Olivera
source_files: 10-22-23 -PM- To Seek the Good of the Church - Psalm 122 - GUEST SPEAKER Pastor Renan de Olivera.txt

outline.title: Introduction: Why Seek the Good of the Church?
subpoints: Companion sermon to the morning message on Psalm 133 (why unity is good and pleasant); this afternoon asks: what are ways in which we can stay united?
Main thesis of Psalm 122: to seek the good of the church is actually to seek your own good; those who commit fully to the worship and life of the church share in its peace and blessing
David's joy (v. 1): rejoicing is the sign of what we are in the Lord; those who rejoice in the gathering of the saints show they are born again

outline.title: Three Characteristics of Jerusalem that Explain Why People Wanted to Stay
subpoints: Characteristic 1 — Jerusalem is compact and indestructible (v. 3): "Jerusalem is built as a city that is compact together" — the church of the New Testament is what Jesus says the gates of hell will not prevail against; as long as the people worship God faithfully there, they are indestructible

Characteristic 2 — The tribes go up together to give thanks (v. 4): Hebrews tells us not to forsake the assembling of ourselves; a tribe could have said "the Philistines will destroy us if we leave" — but the command to gather was given anyway; every excuse not to gather weakens the unity of the church

Characteristic 3 — The thrones of David are set there for judgment (v. 5): the most important reason is that Jerusalem is where the throne of Christ the King is; to stay away from the church is to say that the rule of Christ over His church does not matter to you; we are under the throne of Christ, and that is the greatest reason to be together

outline.title: Seeking the Good of the Church Is Seeking Your Own Good (vv. 6-9)
subpoints: If you are completely committed to the church — to the city, to its peace — then the peace of Jerusalem is very important to you personally; you share in its blessing

Household analogy: if you ask God to bless your home with peace and prosperity, you benefit from that peace; if you do not commit to the life of your family but still want to be blessed, you are looking for frustration
"Pray for the peace of Jerusalem" (v. 6): prayer together is an essential expression of seeking the church's good; it unites us with one another in Christ

Verse 7-8: peace for the walls (where the common people live) and prosperity within the palaces (the most luxurious places) — David prays for all levels of society; "for the sake of my brethren and companions I will now say, Peace be within you" — he prays not for his own benefit but for the sake of others
Verse 9: "Because of the house of the Lord our God, I will seek your good" — the worship of God is the center; seeking the good of the church flows from commitment to worship
The Psalm begins and ends with the same subject — the house of the Lord; everything in the context of worship is what favors our union

outline.title: Conclusion: The Church as the New Jerusalem
subpoints: Jerusalem is no longer the most important physical place; the church is now the new Jerusalem of God, and all who are in the church of Christ receive the benefits of covenant life — peace, unity, and blessing
We are united with Christ by the Spirit, and in His kingdom there is peace and prosperity unto the glory of God the Father
Practical application: prioritize worship; show up; pray for the church; seek the good of the brethren not for personal gain but because of the house of the Lord our God

summary:
In the afternoon sermon, Guest Pastor Renan de Olivera preaches from Psalm 122, the companion text to his morning message on Psalm 133. Where the morning asked *why* unity is good and pleasant, this afternoon addresses *how* we can stay united — specifically, by seeking the good of the church. The sermon's central argument is that to seek the good of the church is ultimately to seek your own good, because those who commit fully to the worship and fellowship of the gathered people receive the blessing that God ordains for that place.

Three characteristics of Jerusalem explain why the pilgrims who came to worship wanted to stay: (1) Jerusalem is compact and indestructible — as long as God's people worship faithfully there, they cannot be destroyed; (2) the tribes go up together to give thanks — no excuse justifies forsaking the assembly, because every such absence weakens the unity of the whole; (3) the thrones of David for judgment are set there — the rule of Christ the King over His church is the most important reason to be present; to absent yourself from the church is to treat Christ's kingship as unimportant.

Pastor de Olivera then applies the prayer of verses 6–9: praying for the peace of Jerusalem is praying for the peace of the church, and those who are committed to the city share in its blessing. He uses a household analogy — if you pray for peace and prosperity for your family but do not actually commit to its life, you are looking for frustration. The Psalm begins and ends with the house of the Lord, showing that everything that favors unity is centered on the worship of God. The church is the new Jerusalem, and all who are in Christ receive all the blessings of covenant life.
transcript:
## Scripture Reading — Psalm 122

A Song of Ascents, of David. I was glad when they said to me, "Let us go into the house of the Lord." Our feet have been standing within your gates, O Jerusalem! Jerusalem is built as a city that is compact together, where the tribes go up, the tribes of the Lord, as a testimony of Israel, to give thanks to the name of the Lord. For thrones are set there for judgment, the thrones of the house of David. Pray for the peace of Jerusalem: "May they prosper who love you. Peace be within your walls, prosperity within your palaces." For the sake of my brethren and companions, I will now say, "Peace be within you." Because of the house of the Lord our God I will seek your good.

Let us pray together. Father, we pray that you anoint your servant this afternoon. Speak through him. Help us to see you clearly. Help us to follow you in faith. Pray that the Spirit will be present with us. For ask in Jesus' name. Amen.

*[Translation provided by Pastor Tito Lyro]*

## Introduction

Good afternoon, brothers. We talked this morning about remaining united together, and basically we talked about the *why* — why we should be united. The reason for that unity is that God abides in the midst of these people, and even with all those problems, He still blesses His people.

What I want to consider this afternoon is about the good of the church. To seek the good of the church is sometimes something that looks like we are doing it for our own good. But what we seek is actually the good of the church, because by seeking the good of the church, we are finding our own good.

David says in verses 1 and 2 of the Psalm that he rejoices because he is going to be in the house of the Lord. Rejoicing in the gathering of the saints is a sign of what we are in the Lord. To rejoice in the things the Lord loves is a sign that we are born again. The one who has no desire for the house of God, who has no joy in being there — that is a warning sign. When we get to the point where we are able to choose those things that please God, we will be able to see clearly what is truly important. And the worship of God is the most important thing for us to maintain our unity. The worship of the church is the greatest tool God uses to keep us together.

When we go about our daily lives, there are many different things that we prefer. Some people like one kind of clothes, others another. Some choose a better car over a nicer house, some the opposite. Some like non-fiction, others like history. But when it comes to worshiping God, that is what every Christian is interested in. Worship has characteristics that make us understand each other, that bring us together — people who otherwise might be divided by personal interests.

And that is why I believe that the one who seeks the good of the church is actually looking for his or her own good. In this place he or she desires to stay.

## Three Characteristics of Jerusalem

Based on the idea that the people wanted to stay in Jerusalem, I want to give you three characteristics that explain why.

**Characteristic 1 — Jerusalem is compact and indestructible (v. 3).** Verse 3 says: "Jerusalem is built as a city that is compact together." This means that the church of the New Testament — where Jesus says the gates of hell will not prevail against the church — is indestructible. If Jerusalem would stand and worship the Lord faithfully, it would be indestructible. The enemies could not destroy them. And that is why the people were so excited and happy to be in Jerusalem — because if they kept on worshiping God faithfully there, they would be strong; they would not be destroyed.

**Characteristic 2 — The tribes go up together to give thanks (v. 4).** Verse 4 says: "Where the tribes go up, the tribes of the Lord, as a testimony of Israel, to give thanks to the name of the Lord." This reminds us of what Hebrews instructs: we are not to forsake the assembling of ourselves, as is the custom of some. A tribe in the time of the Old Testament could have said: "We are here, and the Philistines are between us and Jerusalem — they will destroy us if we leave our homes to go to the feast." That would be an understandable justification. But the command to gather was still given.

People use similar justifications today. In places with genuine persecution — favelas in Brazil where there are criminals and bullets flying — people use danger as a reason not to go to church. But if everyone uses some sort of justification for why it is too dangerous or there is something else to do, and they do not congregate, then the unity of the church is gone. That is why the first and second commandments deal directly with the worship of God. So those who are the people of God — we cannot allow excuses to keep us from being together. God is present in the midst of His people when His people worship Him.

**Characteristic 3 — The thrones of David are set there for judgment (v. 5).** This is the most important. Verse 5: "For thrones are set there for judgment, the thrones of the house of David." We are to maintain the unity. And we can only do that when we are under the throne of Christ — who commands the church and reigns over her.

The thrones of David, the thrones of justice and righteousness, are the thrones of Christ. How does Christ command the church? Through the ministry of the Word. Through the sacraments. Through His officers. And it is in the gathered assembly that Christ the King exercises His rule most visibly. To stay away from the church, to not make the church a priority, is to say that the rule of Christ over His church does not matter to you. We cannot have a greater reason to be united together than that we are under the throne of Christ.

## Seeking the Good of the Church Is Seeking Your Own Good (vv. 6–9)

Now, seeking this good — since we are under the throne of Christ, since we are reunited because we must congregate, since we are here within a reality created by God that is indestructible — now we are going to do certain things. The prayer there, in verse 6, is going to make a lot more sense.

If you are part of this city, if you are completely involved, if you are part of this people, then the peace of Jerusalem is very important to you. Let me give you an example. In your house, you are completely committed to your family. When you ask God to bless your home with peace and prosperity, you benefit from that peace. If your house is blessed with peace, you will be blessed with peace. You come home from work and there is peace in the home — that peace includes you. But if you do not commit to the life of your family, if you do not read the Bible with your children, if you do not pray with your wife, and yet you say, "God, bless my family" — and then your kids grow up and bring shame — you were looking for frustration. You cannot expect peace if you are not seeking the good of the household.

In the same way, if you have no commitment to the worship of God and the people of God, you should not be expecting prosperity and peace in your life. To seek the best for your brethren is to seek the best for you.

Verse 6: "Pray for the peace of Jerusalem: may they prosper who love you." The command, even from the beginning, is to pray — to pray for the peace of Jerusalem. And prayer is also important for being together with one another in Christ. You want unity in the church? You must be present together. And you must pray for one another.

Verses 7–8: "Peace be within your walls, prosperity within your palaces; for the sake of my brethren and companions, I will now say, 'Peace be within you.'" He prays for peace from the walls (where the common people lived) to the palaces (the most luxurious places). And then he gives the reason: not "I pray for Jerusalem because it is good for me," but "for the sake of my brethren and companions." He prays for others. That is the heart of it — seeking the good of the brethren, not seeking your own benefit.

And the companions here are not just the best friends. The companion is the neighbor, the one who is not the closest person in your life but is still in your life. The person you walk with on the edge, on the periphery of your life — he wants them blessed too. From the palace to the walls, from the close friend to the mere acquaintance — he seeks the good of them all.

Verse 9: "Because of the house of the Lord our God, I will seek your good." The worship of God is the center. Seeking the good of the church flows from commitment to the house of the Lord. The Psalm begins in verse 1 with "I was glad when they said to me, let us go into the house of the Lord" — and it ends in verse 9 with "because of the house of the Lord our God, I will seek your good." The Psalm begins and ends with the same subject. The house of the Lord is what frames everything. Everything that happens in the context of worship — everything that is in the house of God — is what favors our union.

So everything that will favor our union must be found within the worship of God. If you want this church to be united, you must be present together.

## Conclusion: The Church as the New Jerusalem

Clearly, Jerusalem is no longer the most important physical place. Jerusalem no longer holds the rule — not the physical Jerusalem. But the church is now the new Jerusalem of God. And now all those who are in the church of Christ are the ones who receive all the benefits that were given by the union, which is the church.

In the same way that in the Old Testament it was those coming to the physical Jerusalem who received that blessing, today it is those who are part of the church of Christ who receive these blessings of peace and unity. As soon as we are in Christ, we are a new creature. We are one in Christ by the Spirit. And Christ, with all in our hearts, makes us the church. And in His kingdom there is peace and prosperity — unto the glory of God the Father.

Amen.

Father in heaven, thank you for your word. Thank you that you are faithful to the new Jerusalem — the Israel of God, the Church of Jesus Christ. Enable us to grow and rejoice in that, that we might be united around that. We pray in Jesus' name. Amen.
